Please use the information below to help me answer questions about biome:

	                                                 Biomes

	Climatic zones are spatial areas of similar climate and weather patterns. Climatic zones are classified by temperature and precipitation data. The study of climatology investigates the geographic controls that produce diverse climate conditions. 

	Biomes are defined as large recognizable groupings of plants and animals. Biomes are also commonly referred to as ecosystems. The distribution of biomes is closely related to certain climates. A biome is named on the basis of its dominant vegetation. 

Tropical Rainforest Biome- Located along the equator in South America, Africa, and Southeast Asia. This biome is warm and humid throughout the year and the trees never experience seasonal leaf fall because the concepts of seasons is unknown in this environment of continuous warmth and moisture. Water surpluses create rivers of enormous discharge such as the Amazon and Congo. 

	The vegetation is a bewildering diversity of plant species displaying a layered structure with a canopy so thick that very little sunlight filters through to the ground. As a result, the ground is relatively clear of vegetation except where trees have fallen or have been cut down creating light gaps where dense jungle growth springs up. The wildlife in the rainforest is largely tree dwellers (arboreal). These animals are creepers, crawlers, climbers, and fliers. This includes monkeys, insects, exotic birds, and reptiles.

	The soils of the tropical rainforest tend to be poor for farming. They are relatively infertile because they have been leached of nutrients by heavy rains. As plant litter accumulates on the forest floor, it is acted upon very rapidly by decomposers and there is a much greater concentration of nutrients in the vegetation than the soil. 

	The tropical rainforests of the world are rapidly shrinking in size due to land use changes. Trees are cut down for their lumber and to clear the land for agriculture and pastures. As much as 40% of the rainforest has been destroyed in South America and Asia. As much as half the rainforest has been destroyed in Africa.

Tropical Deciduous Forest - This biome has a wet season and a dry season that lasts a month or more. The wet season is caused by has abundant rainfall brought on by the Intertropical Convergent Zone. The dry season is caused by the shifting of the Intertropical Convergent Zone out of the area and the descending air of the Subtropical High. 

	The trees drop their leaves during the dry season. There is more undergrowth on the forest floor. There is also not a layered canopy. There fewer arboreal animals and less species diversity overall.  In this biome there starts to be more ground dwelling animals.  

Tropical Scrub- Has the same type of climate that the Tropical Savanna biome has. Tropical Scrub is a woodland not a grassland. It is dominated by short, scraggly trees with thorns. Tropical Scrub is a transition zone between the tropical forests and grasslands.     

Tropical Savanna- Located immediately to the north and south of the tropical rainforests. The key characteristic of this area is a pronounced wet and dry season due again to the movement of the Intertropical Convergence Zone. Temperatures vary more than anywhere else in the tropics. Savanna vegetation consists of a mixture of widely dispersed trees with extensive grasslands, often referred to as parkland. The accumulation of dry grass provides abundant fuel and most parts of the savannas are adapted to annual, natural wildfires. 

Savanna animals vary from continent to continent. The African savannas are the premier “big  game” lands of the world with unmatched richness of large hoofed animals and carnivores. This includes elephants, wildebeests, zebras, lions, and cheetahs. Poaching and habitat losses are quickly reducing the number of wildlife.

Desert- This biome typically receives less than 10 inches of rainfall per year. Sometimes they will receive no rain in a year or receive it all in one great downpour. Deserts also tend to have a very large daily temperature range. A location in the Sahara Desert once recorded a 25 degrees low temperature and a high temperature of 125 degrees in a 24- hour period! This is due to the lack of cloud cover and low amount of water vapor in the air.  

Desert vegetation is xerophytic, or drought resistant. It does not provide a continuous cover; there are patches of bare ground around each plant, whose spreading roots claim any water that exists below the surface. Other adaptations to lack of moisture include, long deep tap roots, thin needles, waxy coating of leaves, spongy interiors for water storage or even dormancy. Very few areas of the desert are completely devoid of vegetation and few are covered with sand dunes. Desert animals are limited by the extreme conditions. 

Mediterranean- A pronounced hot, dry summer/wet, rainy winter regime is typical of Mediterranean climate. Total annual rainfall is approximately 15-20 inches. The typical vegetation is a dense growth of woody shrubs, trees, and grasses known as chaparral in North America and having other names in other areas. In Southern California, a sister plant community called Coastal Sage Scrub is found in lower elevations. The stunted, shrubby growth has a leathery or waxy coating and other adaptations to limit moisture loss. This climate is extremely well suited to the cultivation of a variety of fruits and vegetables and herding livestock. 

Midlatitude Grassland-Is a transition zone between arid and humid areas. The annual precipitation is insufficient to support forest growth. The term prairie is applied to wetter areas where grasses grow taller. The decaying grass makes excellent humus, contributing to the dark color and fertility of grassland soils.

 The natural grasslands were replaced with domesticated grains. Areas with hot summers have more corn, soybeans, hog, and cattle production. Areas with cool summers concentrate on poultry, flax, sunflowers, sugar beets, wheat, and potatoes.  This area has been heavily modified by humans and is the world’s breadbasket. 

Midlatitude Deciduous Forest- Trees vary considerably from region to region and you can find stands of pines as well as broad leaved trees such as oaks, hickory, maple, beech, and others. Areas on the east coast of continents have long growing seasons and plentiful rainfall. These are important agricultural areas and the forests have been heavily exploited by people. Remnants of deer, elk, bear, moose, and wolf populations are found in the northern regions. 

	Areas of Marine West Coast climate have magnificent evergreen forests. The mild winters and summers produce lush forests. These forests have huge ferns and thick undergrowth. The tallest trees in the world are found in this biome. These trees can exceed 1,500 years in age and be 330 feet tall. There is no green without gray and these areas are dominated by coastal fog. 

Boreal Forest- Located in the high latitudes of North America and Eurasia. Boreal Forest is covered with stands of evergreen trees. These far northern forest-clad regions in Siberia are often referred to as Taiga. Poor drainage is typical in summer, due to partially frozen subsoil called permafrost. Normal surface drainage has also been deranged due to previous glaciations. The soils are thinner and less fertile than other biomes. Only a small percentage of the world’s population lives in this climate region and agricultural potential is poor, but it posses important extraction industries (mining, tapping, lumbering). 

Tundra- Located in high latitudes largely north of the Arctic Circle in Alaska, Canada, Scandinavia, and Russia. The land surface is underlain with permafrost. These areas are under continuous snow for 8-10 months of the year. Tundra areas are essentially cold deserts because they receive so little precipitation. These areas get less than 4 inches of precipitation a year. 

Plants that occur briefly when the snow melts and are often low-growing, ground hugging mosses and lichens.  Tundra has a short summer season, but is home in the summer to legions of mosquitoes and black gnats. Alpine tundra is located at high elevations in middle latitudes. Tundra vegetation is very fragile and it takes years to restore itself once it is destroyed. This was a major concern of environmentalists at the time of the construction of the Alaska pipeline. Global climate change is bringing changes to the tundra and its plants, animals, and permafrost. The Arctic is warming at twice the rate of the globe. Melting permafrost is releasing large amounts of methane.

Based on the information provided, here are potential questions and answers related to the biomes described:

1. **What defines a biome?**
   - A biome is defined as a large recognizable grouping of plants and animals, named on the basis of its dominant vegetation. The distribution of biomes is closely related to specific climates.

2. **Why are tropical rainforest soils poor for farming?**
   - Tropical rainforest soils tend to be poor for farming because they are relatively infertile, having been leached of nutrients by heavy rainfalls. There is a greater concentration of nutrients in the vegetation than in the soil due to rapid decomposition.

3. **What causes the wet and dry seasons in the Tropical Deciduous Forest?**
   - The wet season is brought on by abundant rainfall from the Intertropical Convergent Zone, while the dry season occurs due to the shifting of the Intertropical Convergent Zone out of the area and the influence of descending air from the Subtropical High.

4. **How does the vegetation in desert biomes adapt to the lack of moisture?**
   - Desert vegetation adapts through various means such as long deep tap roots, thin needles, a waxy coating on leaves, spongy interiors for water storage, or dormancy to survive the extreme conditions of low moisture.

5. **What is the significance of the Mediterranean climate for agriculture?**
   - The Mediterranean climate, with its hot, dry summers and wet, rainy winters, is extremely well suited to the cultivation of a variety of fruits and vegetables and herding livestock due to its specific weather patterns.

6. **Describe the impact of human activities on the Midlatitude Grassland.**
   - Human activities have heavily modified the Midlatitude Grassland, replacing natural grasslands with domesticated grains such as corn, soybeans, and wheat, effectively making it the world’s breadbasket.

7. **What are the characteristics of the Boreal Forest, and why is its agricultural potential poor?**
   - The Boreal Forest is covered with evergreen trees and experiences poor drainage due to partially frozen subsoil, called permafrost. Soils are thinner and less fertile than in other biomes, contributing to poor agricultural potential.

8. **How is the Alpine tundra different from the Arctic tundra?**
   - While both are characterized by low-growing vegetation, the Alpine tundra is located at high elevations in middle latitudes, different from the Arctic tundra, which is situated in high latitudes north of the Arctic Circle. Both face environmental challenges but are distinguished by their geographic location.

9. **What are the environmental concerns associated with the Tundra biome?**
   - Concerns include the fragility of tundra vegetation, which takes years to restore once destroyed, implications of construction projects like the Alaska pipeline, and the impacts of global climate change, particularly warming rates and melting permafrost releasing methane.

10. **Why are the tropical rainforests rapidly shrinking in size?**
    - Tropical rainforests are rapidly diminishing due to land use changes, with trees being cut down for lumber and to clear land for agricultural and pastoral purposes. Significant portions have been destroyed across South America, Asia, and Africa.
Which biome is the tropical wet climatic zone most associated with

The Tropical Wet climatic zone is most associated with the Tropical Rainforest Biome. This biome is characterized by its location around the equator, notably in regions of South America, Africa, and Southeast Asia. It features a warm and humid climate throughout the year, with high levels of precipitation that do not allow for a seasonal leaf fall. The continuous warmth and moisture support a diverse and dense vegetation structure, with a thick canopy that limits sunlight reaching the ground, fostering a unique ecosystem rich in wildlife, particularly arboreal species.
Which climatic zone is most closely associated with the tropical scrub and tropical savanna biomes?

The Tropical Scrub and Tropical Savanna biomes are most closely associated with the Tropical Wet and Dry climatic zone. This climatic zone features a pronounced wet season and a distinct dry season, largely influenced by the movement of the Intertropical Convergence Zone. The variation in rainfall during the year distinguishes these biomes from more consistently wet environments, like the tropical rainforests. The Tropical Savanna experiences more variation in temperature compared to other tropical zones, characterized by grasslands with dispersed trees that have adapted to both seasonal fires and variable precipitation. Meanwhile, the Tropical Scrub is typified by short, thorny trees and is considered a transition zone between forests and grasslands, also within this same climatic zone.

When answering questions regarding climate zones please use the options below:

Tropical Wet Climate
Tropical Wet climate characteristically occur in an east-west astride the equator, extending 5-10 degrees poleward on either side. The tradewind convergence in the ITCZ leads to widespread uplift of warm, humid air. A Sun high in the sky throughout the year makes for relatively consistent sunlight throughout the year, and so there is little opportunity for seasonal temperature variation.  

The single most descriptive word that can be applied to the tropical wet climate is monotonous, because it is a seasonless climate, with endless repetition of the same weather day after day. The terms summer and winter do not apply because the annual temperature range is miniscule. Tropical Wet climate has the smallest temperature range of any climate type. This seasonless condition has given rise to the saying, “Night is the winter of the tropics.”

	Daily temperature variations are somewhat greater than annual ones, although still not impressive. This is one of the few climates in which the average daily temperature range exceeds the average annual temperature range. On a typical afternoon, the temperature rises to the high 80˚s, dropping to the low 70˚s in the coolest period before dawn. The temperature rarely extends much in the mid 90˚s, even on the hottest days, and equally unusual much cooling at night. 

	Regardless of the thermometer reading, however, the weather feels warm in this climate because high humidity makes the temperatures feel much warmer. Rain can be expected just about every day-sometimes twice or three times a day. Rainfall is of the unstable, showery variety, usually coming from thunderstorms that yield heavy rain for a short time. Clouds develop into thunderstorms producing furious rainfall in the afternoon. The clouds usually disappear by sunset, but redevelop overnight producing nocturnal thunderstorms. Tropical Wet climate gets 60-100 inches of rainfall in a year. Yearly rainfall in the Tropical Wet climate is exceeded by that of the Tropical Monsoon climate. 
   
Tropical Savanna Climate
	Tropical Savanna climate is the most extensive of the tropical climates. Tropical Savanna climate generally is poleward of the Tropical Wet climate. The poleward limits of Tropical Savanna climate are approximately equivalent to the poleward maximum migration of the ITCZ. The distinctive characteristic of Tropical Savanna climate is a clear-cut seasonal alteration between wet and dry periods. Annual rainfall totals in the Tropical Savanna climate are generally less than in the other two tropical climates. The summer is conspicuously wet, with one to four months receiving at least 10 inches of rain. The winter season, on the other hand, is distinctly a time of drought, sometimes with three or four rainless months.  
	
	As the ITCZ shifts latitudinally with the Sun over the course of the year, Tropical Savanna regions experience extreme contrasts in weather. During the winter, the ITCZ shifts toward the opposite hemisphere so that Tropical Savanna regions are dominated by the subtropical high-pressure conditions of subsiding air which produce clear skies. In summer, the system shifts in the opposite direction, bringing the ITCZ and its wet tropical weather patterns into the Tropical Savanna region. 

	Average annual temperatures in Tropical Savanna are about the same as in Tropical Wet regions, but with somewhat greater month-to-month variation. Winter is a little cooler and summer is a little hotter than in the Tropical Wet regions, giving an annual temperature range of 5 to 15 degrees. As in the Tropical Monsoon climate, the hottest time of the year is likely to be in late spring just before the onset of the summer rains.    

Tropical Monsoon Climate
	The distinctiveness of the Tropical Monsoon climate is shown primarily in its rainfall pattern. During the summer, an enormous amount of rain falls in association with the summer monsoon. It is not unusual to have more than 30 inches of rain in each of the two or three months. The annual precipitation is between 100 to 200 inches of rain a year. During the winter the Tropical Monsoon climate produces little precipitation. A location may get less than 2.5 inches a rainfall a month and one or two months may be rainless. 

	A lesser distinction of the Tropical Monsoon is its annual temperature range. Although the annual temperature range may be only slightly greater than in a Tropical Wet climate, the highest Tropical Monsoon temperatures normally occur in late spring prior to the onset of the summer monsoon. The heavy cloud cover of the wet monsoon period shields out some of the heat of the sun, resulting in slightly lower temperatures in the summer than in the spring. 

Subtropical Desert Climate 
	In both the Northern and Southern Hemispheres, subtropical deserts lie either in or very near the band of subtropical highs. Arid conditions reach to the western coasts of all continents in these latitudes. Subtropical Desert climates do not occur on the east coast of continents because the western side of the subtropical high is weaker. The distinctive climatic characteristic of deserts is the lack of moisture and three adjectives are particularly applicable to precipitation conditions in the subtropical deserts:

Scarce- Subtropical Deserts are among the driest places on earth. Many areas within Subtropical Deserts experience several consecutive years without a single drop of moisture falling from the sky. Most Subtropical Deserts are not totally without precipitation. Annual totals of between 2 and 8 inches are characteristic, and some places receive as much as 15 inches. 

Unreliable- The very concept of an “average” yearly rainfall in a Subtropical Desert is misleading because of year-to-year fluctuations. Yuma, Arizona has a long-term average rainfall of 2.7 inches, but within the last decade it has received as little as 0.5 inches and as much as 7 inches in a given year. Deviation from the norm tends to be very high in Subtropical Desert Climates. 

Intense- Most precipitation in these regions falls in vigorous convective showers that are localized and short in duration. Thus, rare rains may bring brief floods to regions that have been bereft of surface moisture for months. 

The combination of low-latitude location and lack of cloudiness permits a great deal of heat to reach the surface. Summers are long and blisteringly hot, with monthly averages in the middle to high 90˚s. Midwinter months have average temperatures in the high 60˚s, which gives moderate annual temperature ranges of 15˚-25˚. Daily temperature ranges, on the other hand are sometimes astounding. Summer days are so hot that the nights do not have time to cool off significantly, but during the transition seasons of spring and fall, a 50˚ fluctuation between the heat of the afternoon and the cool of the following dawn is not unusual. 





Midlatitude Desert Climate
	Midlatitude Desert climates occur primarily in the deep interiors of continents, where they are either far removed geographically or blocked from oceanic influence. Precipitation in Midlatitude Deserts is much like that in Subtropical Deserts- meager and erratic. Differences lie in two aspects: seasonality and intensity. Most Midlatitude Deserts receive the bulk of their precipitation in summer. Winter is usually dominated by low temperatures and dry conditions. Although most precipitation is of the showery variety, there are also some periods of general overcast and protracted drizzle.

	The principle climatic differences between Midlatitude and Subtropical Deserts are in temperature, especially winter temperatures, with Midlatitude Deserts having severely cold winters. The average cold-month temperature is normally below freezing, and some Midlatitude Deserts have six months with below freezing-averages. This cold produces average annual temperatures that are much lower than in Subtropical Deserts and this greatly increases the annual temperature range. Variations exceeding 50˚ between January and July average temperatures are common.

Mediterranean Climate
	Mediterranean climate is found on the western side of continents centered at about 35˚ north and south latitude. The genesis of Mediterranean climates is clear-cut. These regions are dominated in summer by dry, subsiding air from the eastern portion of the subtropical high. In winter, the wind and pressure belts shift equatorword, and Mediterranean regions come under the influence of the westerlies. Almost all precipitation comes from the westerlies. Mediterranean climate has three distinctive characteristics:

The modest annual precipitation falls in winter, summers being virtually rainless.
Winter temperatures are usually mild for the midlatitudes, and summer varies from hot to warm.
Clear skies and abundant sunshine are typical especially in summer.

Average annual precipitation is modest ranging from 15 inches on the equatorward margin to about 25 inches at the poleward margin of the climatic zone. The midwinter rainfall is from 3 to 5 inches per month and two or three midsummer months are totally dry. Only Marine West Coast climate has such a concentration of precipitation in winter. 

Mediterranean climate has summers that are hot, with midsummer monthly averages between 75˚ and 85˚ and a maximum of 100˚. Average cold-month temperatures are about 50˚, with occasional minimums below freezing. Coastal areas have milder temperatures and a smaller temperature range. 

Humid Subtropical Climate
	Whereas Mediterranean climates are found on the western side of continents, the climate at the same latitude but on the eastern side is classified as Humid Subtropical. Humid Subtropical climate differs from Mediterranean climate in several important respects. Summer temperatures in the Humid Subtropical climate are generally warm to hot, with the highest monthly averages between 75˚ and 80˚. This is not dissimilar to the situation in the Mediterranean climate, but the Humid Subtropical regions are characterized by much higher humidity in summer. Day time tends to be hot and sultry, and often night brings little relief.

Winter temperatures are mild on average, but winter is punctuated by cold waves that bring severe weather for a few days at a time. Minimum temperatures in the Humid Subtropical climate can be 10˚-20˚ lower than in the Mediterranean climate, which means that killing frosts can occur. For example, the northernmost limit of commercial citrus crop in the eastern United States is 625 miles farther north than in the western United States. 

Annual precipitation is generally abundant, with a general decrease from east to west. Averages are mostly between 40 and 60 inches. Summer is usually the time that the Humid Subtropical climate gets the most precipitation. The east coast of North America and Asia can get large amounts of rainfall from the Hurricanes and Typhoons that form over the warm waters of east coast locations. Winter has less precipitation but is not dry.   
      
Marine West Coast Climate
	As the name implies, Marine West Coast climates are situated on the western side of continents between 40˚ and 65˚ latitude. These regions are influenced by the westerlies throughout the year, and the persistent onshore wind creates a lack of extreme temperature, much cloudiness, and high proportions of days with some precipitation. 

 	Average hot-month temperatures are generally between 60˚-70˚, with cold months averaging between 35˚ and 45˚. There can be very hot days and cold days in a Marine West Coast climate but it is rare and it only lasts for a couple days. There is an abnormally long growing season for this latitude. Around Seattle the growing season is a month longer than the area around Atlanta even though Atlanta is over a thousand miles south of Seattle. 

	Marine West Coast climate areas receive 30 to 50 inches of precipitation a year. Much higher totals are found on the windward slopes of mountains. Rainfall probability in the winter time is high, but the intensity is low. Drizzly precipitation is characteristic. Humidity is high, with much cloudiness. 

Humid Continental Climate
	Humid Continental Climate is dominated by the westerly wind belt throughout the year, which means there is frequent weather changes associated with the passage of the jet stream, especially in winter. Variability, both seasonal and daily, is a prominent characteristic. Humid Continental climate gets cold waves, heat waves, blizzards, thunderstorms, and tornadoes.  

	Hot-month temperatures generally average in the mid 70˚s, and so summers are as warm as those of the Humid Subtropical climate in the south, although the summer is shorter. The average cold-month temperature is between 10˚ and 25˚, with one to five months averaging below freezing. 

	Annual precipitation totals average between 20 and 40 inches. Summer is the wetter time of year, but winter is not totally dry. Summer rain comes mostly from thunderstorms. Winter precipitation is associated with the jet stream, and much of it falls as snow. 

Subarctic Climate
	Annual temperature ranges in the Subarctic climate are the largest in the world. Variations from hot-month temperature and cold-month temperatures can exceed 100˚. Spring and Fall are brief transition seasons that slip by rapidly, usually in April/May and September/October. Summer is short and winter is dominant.           

The key word in the subarctic climate is winter, which is long, dark, and bitterly cold. In most places, ice begins to form on the lakes in September or October and does not thaw until May or later. For six or seven months, the average temperature is below freezing and the coldest months have averages below -36˚. Summer warms up remarkably despite its short duration. Although the intensity of the sunlight is low (because of the low solar angle), summer days are very long. Summer nights are too short to permit too much cooling. Average hot-month temperatures are typically in the high 50˚s and low 60˚s. 

	Precipitation is usually meager in the subarctic climate. Annual totals range from 5 inches to 20 inches. Despite these sparse evaporation rates, the evaporation is low and the soil is frozen for much of the year so that the moisture is adequate to support a forest. Summer is the wet season and the precipitation comes in the form of thunderstorms. Winter experiences only light snowfall which may accumulate to 2 to 3 feet in depth. The snow that falls on the ground in October is still likely to be on the ground in May, because very little of it melts in winter. Thus, a continuous thin snow covers exists for months despite the sparseness of actual snow fall.

Average annual precipitation is modest ranging from 15 inches on the equatorward margin to about 25 inches at the poleward margin of the climatic zone. The midwinter rainfall is from 3 to 5 inches per month and two or three midsummer months are totally dry. Only Marine West Coast climate has such a concentration of precipitation in winter. 

Mediterranean climate has summers that are hot, with midsummer monthly averages between 75˚ and 85˚ and a maximum of 100˚. Average cold-month temperatures are about 50˚, with occasional minimums below freezing. Coastal areas have milder temperatures and a smaller temperature range. 

Humid Subtropical Climate
	Whereas Mediterranean climates are found on the western side of continents, the climate at the same latitude but on the eastern side is classified as Humid Subtropical. Humid Subtropical climate differs from Mediterranean climate in several important respects. Summer temperatures in the Humid Subtropical climate are generally warm to hot, with the highest monthly averages between 75˚ and 80˚. This is not dissimilar to the situation in the Mediterranean climate, but the Humid Subtropical regions are characterized by much higher humidity in summer. Day time tends to be hot and sultry, and often night brings little relief.

Winter temperatures are mild on average, but winter is punctuated by cold waves that bring severe weather for a few days at a time. Minimum temperatures in the Humid Subtropical climate can be 10˚-20˚ lower than in the Mediterranean climate, which means that killing frosts can occur. For example, the northernmost limit of commercial citrus crop in the eastern United States is 625 miles farther north than in the western United States. 

Annual precipitation is generally abundant, with a general decrease from east to west. Averages are mostly between 40 and 60 inches. Summer is usually the time that the Humid Subtropical climate gets the most precipitation. The east coast of North America and Asia can get large amounts of rainfall from the Hurricanes and Typhoons that form over the warm waters of east coast locations. Winter has less precipitation but is not dry.   
      
Marine West Coast Climate
	As the name implies, Marine West Coast climates are situated on the western side of continents between 40˚ and 65˚ latitude. These regions are influenced by the westerlies throughout the year, and the persistent onshore wind creates a lack of extreme temperature, much cloudiness, and high proportions of days with some precipitation. 

 	Average hot-month temperatures are generally between 60˚-70˚, with cold months averaging between 35˚ and 45˚. There can be very hot days and cold days in a Marine West Coast climate but it is rare and it only lasts for a couple days. There is an abnormally long growing season for this latitude. Around Seattle the growing season is a month longer than the area around Atlanta even though Atlanta is over a thousand miles south of Seattle. 

	Marine West Coast climate areas receive 30 to 50 inches of precipitation a year. Much higher totals are found on the windward slopes of mountains. Rainfall probability in the winter time is high, but the intensity is low. Drizzly precipitation is characteristic. Humidity is high, with much cloudiness. 

Humid Continental Climate
	Humid Continental Climate is dominated by the westerly wind belt throughout the year, which means there is frequent weather changes associated with the passage of the jet stream, especially in winter. Variability, both seasonal and daily, is a prominent characteristic. Humid Continental climate gets cold waves, heat waves, blizzards, thunderstorms, and tornadoes.  

	Hot-month temperatures generally average in the mid 70˚s, and so summers are as warm as those of the Humid Subtropical climate in the south, although the summer is shorter. The average cold-month temperature is between 10˚ and 25˚, with one to five months averaging below freezing. 

	Annual precipitation totals average between 20 and 40 inches. Summer is the wetter time of year, but winter is not totally dry. Summer rain comes mostly from thunderstorms. Winter precipitation is associated with the jet stream, and much of it falls as snow. 

Subarctic Climate
	Annual temperature ranges in the Subarctic climate are the largest in the world. Variations from hot-month temperature and cold-month temperatures can exceed 100˚. Spring and Fall are brief transition seasons that slip by rapidly, usually in April/May and September/October. Summer is short and winter is dominant.           

The key word in the subarctic climate is winter, which is long, dark, and bitterly cold. In most places, ice begins to form on the lakes in September or October and does not thaw until May or later. For six or seven months, the average temperature is below freezing and the coldest months have averages below -36˚. Summer warms up remarkably despite its short duration. Although the intensity of the sunlight is low (because of the low solar angle), summer days are very long. Summer nights are too short to permit too much cooling. Average hot-month temperatures are typically in the high 50˚s and low 60˚s. 

	Precipitation is usually meager in the subarctic climate. Annual totals range from 5 inches to 20 inches. Despite these sparse evaporation rates, the evaporation is low and the soil is frozen for much of the year so that the moisture is adequate to support a forest. Summer is the wet season and the precipitation comes in the form of thunderstorms. Winter experiences only light snowfall which may accumulate to 2 to 3 feet in depth. The snow that falls on the ground in October is still likely to be on the ground in May, because very little of it melts in winter. Thus, a continuous thin snow covers exists for months despite the sparseness of actual snow fall.

The Tropical Scrub and Tropical Savanna biomes are most closely associated with the Tropical Savanna Climate. This climate is characterized by a clear-cut seasonal alteration between wet and dry periods, with the wet season bringing significant rainfall and the dry season experiencing drought conditions. The Tropical Savanna climate is typically found poleward of the Tropical Wet climate, with its geographical range roughly corresponding to the maximum latitudinal shift of the Intertropical Convergence Zone (ITCZ). This results in extreme contrasts in weather throughout the year, with summer being conspicuously wet and winter distinctly dry.
Why does the tropical scrub and tropical savanna biomes lack extensive forests (such as those found in the tropical rainforest and tropical deciduous forest biomes)?

The Tropical Scrub and Tropical Savanna biomes lack extensive forests, such as those found in the tropical rainforest and tropical deciduous forest biomes, primarily due to their distinct climate conditions, particularly the clear-cut seasonal variation between wet and dry periods characteristic of the Tropical Savanna Climate.
    
1. **Seasonal Rainfall and Drought**: Unlike the constant, high rainfall seen in tropical rainforests, Tropical Savanna climates have a pronounced wet season followed by a prolonged dry season. The dry season significantly limits the ability of trees to grow extensively as it does in more consistently humid environments like rainforests. The lack of water for part of the year restricts the types of vegetation that can survive, favoring grasses and scattered trees or shrubs that can withstand periods of drought.

2. **Soil Conditions**: The soil in Tropical Savanna and Tropical Scrub biomes often lacks the nutrients found in the richer soils of the rainforests. Periodic fires during the dry season in savannas also contribute to a different soil composition. While these fires help maintain the grassland ecosystem by clearing away dead plant material and returning nutrients to the soil, they can prevent the establishment of large areas of forest.

3. **Fire Adaptation and Suppression**: Many of the grasses and plants in the savanna are adapted to, and even reliant on, periodic fires for their lifecycle. These fires help suppress tree growth by periodically burning saplings and clearing out underbrush, thereby maintaining the dominance of grasslands and preventing the establishment of extensive forests.

4. **Water Availability**: Trees require significant amounts of water to thrive, and the erratic rainfall of the savanna climates does not consistently provide the necessary moisture for dense forests to develop. The extended dry periods can be particularly challenging for saplings and young trees, which might not have the deep root systems needed to access deeper groundwater sources.

5. **Evapotranspiration**: High temperatures and potential evapotranspiration rates in these biomes can further exacerbate water stress on vegetation, favoring drought-resistant species over those requiring more water, thus limiting extensive forest growth.

In summary, the combination of less consistent rainfall, periodic droughts, soil conditions, the role of fire in the ecosystem, and adaptations of local flora to water scarcity play crucial roles in why extensive forests like those in the tropical rainforest and tropical deciduous forest biomes are not typically found in the Tropical Scrub and Tropical Savanna biomes.

Why are there no large areas of boreal forest and tundra in the Southern Hemisphere?

In the Southern Hemisphere, there are no large expanses of land at the latitudes corresponding to the boreal forest and tundra biomes found in the Northern Hemisphere. Most of the land in the Southern Hemisphere at these latitudes is either ocean or part of Antarctica, which has a polar climate. Despite the implications of the well-known phrase “as common as dirt,” soil is remarkably diverse. Soil is a relatively thin surface layer made up of a mixture of eroded mineral particles, decaying organic matter, living organisms, gases, and liquid. Soil acts as a fundamental interface where the atmosphere, hydrosphere, biosphere, and lithosphere meet. Soil has been called the skin of the earth.

Soil is a relatively thin surface layer of mineral matter that normally contains a considerable amount of organic material and is capable of supporting living plants. Almost all land plants sprout from soil which is thinly spread across the earth’s surface. Soil extends from the surface down to a maximum depth to which living organisms penetrate, which means basically the area occupied by the plant’s roots. 
	
Soil is an often inconspicuous component of the landscape because it is normally masked by vegetation, roads, parking lots, buildings, and so on. We recognize soil in the landscape mostly by its color. The depth of the soil layer becomes obvious only where it is exposed by deep erosion or a road cut. 

From Regolith to Soil
Soil development begins with the physical and chemical disintegration of rock exposed to the atmosphere and to the action of the water percolating down from the surface. This disintegration is called weathering. The basic result of weathering is the breakdown and weakening of solid rock and the fragmentation of rock masses.  

The principle product of weathering is a layer of loose inorganic material called regolith (blanket rock) because it lies like a blanket over the unfragmented bedrock below. The regolith consists of materials that have weathered from the underlying rock. Regolith has a crude gradation of particle sizes, with the largest and least fragmented pieces at the bottom, immediately adjacent to the bedrock. Sometimes the regolith consists of material that was transported from elsewhere by the action of wind, water, or ice. This regolith may vary significantly in composition from place to place. 

The very upper portion of the regolith normally differs from the material below in several ways, most notably in the intensity of the biological and chemical processes taking place. Soil is mostly composed of finely fragmented mineral particles and is the ultimate product of weathering. It normally contains an abundance of living plant roots, dead and rotting plant parts, microscopic plants and animals (both living and dead), and a variable amount of air and water. 

Soil is not the end product, but rather a stage in a never ending continuum of physical, chemical, and biological processes. Soil is an ever-evolving material. Soil acts like a sponge-taking in inputs and being acted upon by the local environment. When the inputs of the soil change or the local environment changes, the soil will change along with it.   

Soil Forming Factors- Geology
The source of the rock fragments that make up soil is the parent material, which may be either bedrock or loose sediments transported from elsewhere by water, wind, or ice. The nature of the parent material often influences the characteristics of the soil that develops from it; this factor sometimes dominates all others, particularly in early stages of soil formation. The chemical composition of the parent material is obviously reflected in the resulting soil, and the parent-material physical characteristics may also be influential in soil development, particularly in terms of texture and structure. 

Bedrock that weathers into large particles (as does sandstone, for example) normally produces a coarse-textured soil, one easily penetrated by air and water to some depth. Bedrock that weathers into minute particles (shale, for example) yields fine-textured soils with a great number of pores but of very small size, which inhibits air and water from easily penetrating the surface. 

Young soils are likely to be reflective of the rocks or sediments from which they were derived. With the passages of time, however, other soil-forming factors become increasingly important and the significance of the parent material diminishes. Eventually the influence of the parent material may be completely obliterated, so that it is sometimes impossible to ascertain the nature of the rock from which the soil evolved.

Soil Forming Factors- Climate
Temperature and moisture are the climatic variables of greatest significance to soil formation. Both chemical and biological processes in soil are usually accelerated by high temperatures and abundant moisture and are slowed by low temperatures and lack of moisture. Soils tend to be deepest in warm humid regions and shallowest in cold dry regions. Climate is likely to be the most influential factor in the long run. 

Soils in middle-latitude grasslands normally have a high proportion of organic matter. Those in deserts are thin and rich in minerals left behind by evaporating water like salts and lime. Tropical soils typically have a high content of iron and aluminum oxides. 

It is difficult to overemphasize the role of moisture moving through the soil. The flow is mostly downward because of the pull of gravity, but is sometimes sideways in response to drainage opportunities and sometimes in special circumstances, even upward. Wherever and however water moves, it always carries dissolved chemicals in solution and usually also carries tiny particles of material in suspension. Moving water is ever engaged in rearranging the chemical and physical components of soil, as well as contributing to the variety and availability of plant nutrients. 

Soil Forming Factors- Topography 
Slope and drainage are the two main features of topography that influence soil characteristics. Wherever soil develops its vertical extent undergoes continuous (usually very slow) change. This change comes about through a lowering of both the bottom and top layer of the soil layer. The bottom slowly gets deeper as weathering penetrates into the regolith and parent material as plant roots extend to greater depths. At the same time, the soil surface is being lowered by the sporadic removal of its uppermost layer through normal erosion (caused mostly running water and wind). 

Where the land is flat, soils tend to develop at the bottom more rapidly than it is eroded away at the top. This does not mean that the downward development is speedy; rather it means that surface erosion is extraordinarily slow. The deepest soils are usually found on flat land. Where slopes are relatively steep, surface erosion is more rapid than soil deepening, with the result that such soils are almost always thin and immaturely developed.  

If soils are well drained, moisture relationships may be relatively unimportant factors in soil development. If soils have poor natural drainage (such as where water is standing on the surface), significantly different characteristics may develop. For example, waterlogged soil tends to contain a high proportion of organic matter, and the biological processes that require biological and chemical processes that require free oxygen are impeded. Air is the source of the needed oxygen and a waterlogged soil contains essentially no air. 

Most poorly drained soils are in valley bottoms or in some other locale because soil drainage is usually related to slope. In some cases, such subsurface factors such as permeability and the presence or absence of impermeable layers are more influential than slope.

Soil Forming Factors- Biology
	By volume, soil is about half mineral matter and about half air and water, with only a small fraction of organic matter. However, this organic fraction (consisting of both living and dead plants and animals) is of utmost importance. The biological factor gives life to the soil and makes it more than just dirt. Every soil contains (sometimes an enormous quantity of) living organisms, and every soil incorporates (sometimes a vast amount of) dead and decaying organic material. 

	Vegetation of various kinds growing in soil performs certain vital functions. Plant roots work their way down and around, providing passageways for drainage and aeration, as well as being the vital link between soil nutrients and growing plants. 

	Many kinds of animals contribute to soil development as well. Even such large surface-dwelling creatures as elephants and bison affect soil formation by compacting the soil with their hooves, rolling in the dirt, grazing on vegetation, and dropping manure. Ants, worms, and all other land animals fertilize the soil with their waste products and contribute their carcasses for eventual decomposition and incorporation into the soil. 

	Many small animals spend most or all their lives in the soil layer (tunneling here and there) moving soil particles upward and downward, and providing passageways for water and air. Mixing and plowing by soil fauna can be remarkably extensive. As ants and termites build mounds, they also transport soil materials from one layer to another. The mixing activities of animals in the soil tend to counteract the tendency of other soil-forming processes to accentuate the vertical differences among soil layers. The organisms within a soil range from microscopic protozoans to larger animals that may accidentally alter certain soil characteristics. 

Of all creatures, the earthworm is probably the most important to soil formation and development. The cultivating and mixing activities of earthworms are of great value in improving the structure, increasing the fertility, lessening the danger of accelerated erosion, and deepening the profile of the soil. The mere presence of earthworms does not guarantee that a soil will be highly productive, as there may be other kinds of inhibiting factors, such as a high water table. Nevertheless, an earthworm-rich soil has a higher potential for productivity compared to similar soils lacking earthworms. In various controlled experiments, the addition of earthworms to wormless soil enhanced plant productivity by several hundred percent. 

In many parts of the world, earthworms are lacking. They are absent from arid and semiarid regions. In these dry lands, some of the earthworm’s soil-enhancing functions are carried out by ants and termites, but much less effectively. The migration of earthworms into new areas is occurring in many areas around the world and their arrival leads to significant changes.         


Soil Forming Factors- Time
For soil to develop on a newly exposed land surface requires time. The time depends on the nature of the exposed parent material and the characteristics of the environment. Soil forming processes are generally very slow, and many centuries may be required for a thin layer of soil to form on a newly exposed surface. A warm, moist environment is conductive to soil development. Normally of much greater importance are the attributes of the parent material. For example, soil develops from sediments relatively quickly and from bedrock relatively slowly. 

Most soil develops with geologic slowness- so slowly that changes are almost imperceptible within a human life span. It is possible for soil to become degraded, either through the physical removal associated with accelerated erosion or through the depletion of nutrients in only a few years. 

Soils that have fine textures (especially those with low rates of rainwater infiltration) tend to be those that are most easily eroded by rainwater runoff and wind. Steep slopes with the lack of vegetation cover also increase the likelihood of erosion. In regions where single-crop agriculture is practiced, fields are often left bare and unplanted for several months each year, increasing the likelihood of erosion. 

In the United States, some researchers estimate that nearly 40% of the productive soil in wheat-growing the Palouse region of Washington and Idaho, and as much as 50% of the topsoil of Iowa, has been lost to erosion over the last 150 years. Globally, perhaps 10 million hectares of cropland are lost each year to soil erosion- 10 to 40 times faster than the rate at which productive soil can develop. It is important to realize that in the grand scale of geologic time, soil can be formed and reformed, but in the dimension of human time, it is a nonrenewable resource.  

Soil Components- Inorganic Materials 
The bulk of most soils are mineral matter mostly in the form of very small particles. About half of the volume of an average soil is small, granular mineral matter called sand and silt. These particles may consist of a great variety of minerals, depending on the nature of the parent material from which they were derived. Sand and silt are simply fragments of the wasting of rock. Most common are bits of quartz, which are composed of silica and appear in the soil as very resistant grains of sand. Other prominent minerals making up sand and silt are feldspars and micas. 

The smallest particles in the soil are clay, which is usually a combination of silica and oxides of aluminum and iron found only in the soil and not in the parent material. Clay has properties significantly different than those of sand and silt fragments. Most clay particles are colloidal in size, which means they are larger than molecules but too small to be seen with the naked eye. Clay particles are usually flat platelets and therefore have a relatively large surface area. 

Clay has an important influence on chemical activities in the soil because many chemical reactions occur at the surface of soil particles. The platelets grow together in loose, sheetlike assemblages, and water moves easily between these sheets. Substances dissolved in the water are attracted to and held by the sheets. Since the sheets are negatively charged, they attract positively charged ions called cations. Many essential plant nutrients occur in soil solutions as cations, with the result that clay is an important reservoir for plant nutrients, just as it is for soil water.

 


Soil Components- Organic Matter
	Although organic matter generally constitutes less than 5% of total soil volume, it has an enormous influence on soil characteristics and plays a fundamental role in the biochemical processes that make soil an effective medium for plant growth. Some of the organic matter is living organisms, some is dead, and some is the undecomposed parts of plants and animal carcasses. 

	Apart from plant roots, evidence of the variety and bounty of organisms living in the soil may not be obvious, but most soils are seething with life. One acre of soil may contain a million earthworms. The total number of microorganisms in one ounce of soil likely exceeds 100 trillion. Microorganisms far exceed larger life-forms, both in total numbers and cumulative mass. They are active in rearranging and aerating the soil and in yielding waste products that are links in the chain of nutrient cycling. Some make major contributions to the decay and decomposition of dead organic matter, and others make nitrogen available for plant use.  

	Leaves, twigs, stalks, and other dead plant parts accumulate at the soil surface, where they are referred to collectively as litter. The eventual fate of most litter is decomposition, in which solid parts are broken down into chemical components, which are then absorbed into the soil or washed away. In cold, dry areas, litter may remain undecomposed for a very long time. In warm, moist areas, the decomposition may take place almost as rapidly as the litter accumulates.

 	Humus is the decayed remains of plant and animals materials, partially transformed by bacterial action. Humus is an important catalyst in chemical reactions that help plants extract soil nutrients. Humus also supplies nutrients and minerals to the soil. Soils that contain humus are quite workable and have a good capacity to retain water. Humus also provides an abundant food source for microscopic soil organisms. 

Most soils are actually environments that teem with life, ranging from microscopic bacteria and fungi, to earthworms, rodents, and other burrowers. Animals contribute to the soil development and enrichment by creating humus from plant litter. They also mix organic material deeper in the soil and move inorganic fragments toward the surface. The functions of plants and their root systems are integral parts of the soil-forming system.  

Soil Components- Air
Nearly half the volume of an average soil is made up of pore spaces. These spaces provide a labyrinth of interconnecting passageways, called interstices, among the soil particles. This labyrinth lets air and water penetrate into the soil. On the average, the pore spaces are about half filled with air and half with water. At any given time and place, the amounts of air and water are quite variable, the quantity of one varying inversely with that of the other. 

The characteristics of air in the soil are significantly different from those of atmospheric air. Soil air is found in openings generally lined with a film of water. Because this air exists in such close contact with the water it is not exposed to moving air currents and it is saturated with water vapor. Soil air is also very rich in carbon dioxide and poor in oxygen because plant roots and soil organisms remove oxygen from, and respire carbon dioxide into the pore spaces. The carbon dioxide then slowly escapes to the atmosphere. 

The problem with water saturated soil is not necessary excess water. If all pore spaces are filled with water, there is no air supply. The lack of air is why many plants find it difficult to survive in water-saturated soils. 

Soil Components- Water
Soil water is not pure, but is a solution that contains soluble nutrients. Plants need air, water, and minerals to live and grow and they depend on soil for much of these necessities. Water comes into the soil largely through the percolation of rainfall and snowmelt. Some is also added from below when groundwater is pulled up from above the water table by capillary action. Once water has penetrated the soil, it covers each solid particle in a film of water or it either wholly or partially fills the pore spaces. Water can be lost from the soil by percolation down into the groundwater, by upward capillary movement to the surface followed by evaporation or plant evaporation (transpiration). 

Gravitational Water (Free Water)
Gravitational water is temporary in that it results from prolonged infiltration from above (usually due to prolonged precipitation) and is pulled downward by gravity, through interstices toward the groundwater zone. This water stays in the soil only a short time and is not very effective in supplying plants because it drains away rapidly once the external supply ceases. The quantity of gravitational water in soil is related to several conditions: the amount of precipitation, the time since it fell, evaporation rates, the space available for storage, and how easily the water can move through the soil. 

Gravitational water performs several functions in a soil. As gravitational water percolates downward, it dissolves soluble minerals and carries them into deeper levels of the soil, perhaps where all open spaces are saturated. Depleting nutrients in the soil through the flow of water is called leaching. 

Capillary Water (Water of Cohesion)
Capillary water remains after gravitational water has drained away. Capillary water is by far the principle source of moisture for plants. It consists of moisture held at the surface of soil particles. Water clumps by surface tension (the property that causes small water droplets to form rounded beads instead of spreading out in a thin film). In this form of soil moisture, the surface tension forces are stronger than the downward pull of gravity. 

Water is free to move about equally well in all directions in response to capillary tension. It tends to move from wetter areas to drier ones, which accounts for the upward movement of capillary water when no gravitational water is percolating downward. Thus, during dry periods when there is no gravitational water flowing through the soil, capillary water can move upward or horizontally to supply plant roots with moisture and dissolved nutrients. 

Capillary water migrates upward and moves minerals from the subsoil toward the surface. If this capillary water evaporates away, the formerly dissolved minerals remain generally as alkaline or saline deposits on the topsoil. High concentrations of certain mineral deposits like these can be detrimental to plants and the existing soil. Lime (calcium carbonate) deposited by evaporating soil water can build up to produce a cementlike layer (called caliche) which like a clay hardpan prevents the downward percolation of water. 



Hygroscopic Water (Water of Adhesion) 
Hygroscopic water consists of a microscopically thin film of moisture bound rigidly to all soil particles by adhesion, which is the attraction of water molecules to solid surfaces. Hygroscopic water adheres so tightly to the particles that it is normally unavailable to plants. It does not move through the soil. 

For plants, capillary water is the most important and gravitational water is largely superfluous. After gravitational water has drained away, the remaining water represents the field capacity of soil. If drought conditions prevail and capillary water is all used up by plants or evaporated, the plants are no longer able to extract moisture from the soil. This is when the wilting point is reached. 

Water performs a number of important functions in the soil. It is an effective solvent, dissolving essential soil nutrients and making them available to plant roots. These dissolved nutrients are carried downward in solution, to be partly redeposited at lower levels. This process is called leaching. Leaching tends to deplete the topsoil of soluble nutrients. Water is required for many chemical reactions of clay and it can have a considerable influence on the physical characteristics of soil by moving particles. 

Eluviation is the downward removal of soil components by water. As gravitational water percolates downward it deposits the fine materials that were removed from the topsoil to the subsoil. One result of eluviation is that the texture of the topsoil tends to become coarser as the fine particles are removed. Consequently the topsoil’s ability to retain water is reduced.

Illuviation is the deposition by water in the subsoil to a lower soil level. Gravitational water also mixes soil particles as it moves them downward. Illuviation may eventually cause the subsoil to become dense and compact forming a clay hardpan. 

The moisture added to the soil by percolation of rainfall or snowmelt is diminished largely through evapotranspiration. The dynamic relationship between these two processes is referred to as the soil-water balance. It is influenced by a variety of factors, including soil and vegetation characteristics, but is primarily determined by temperature and humidity. 

How much water is available to plants is much more important to an ecosystem than is the amount of precipitation. Much of the water derived from rainfall or snowmelt becomes unavailable to plants because of evaporation, runoff, deep infiltration, or other processes. At any given time there is likely to be either a surplus or deficit of water in the soil. Such a condition is only temporary, and it varies in response to changing weather conditions particularly those related to seasonal change.

January is a time of surplus water in the soil because low temperatures inhibit evaporation and transpiration. Also, the plants are either dead or in a state of hibernation. The soil is likely to be near field capacity at this time, which means most of the pore spaces are filled with water. With the arrival of spring, temperatures rise and plant growth accelerates. The soil-water balance tips from a water surplus to a water deficit. Heavy use and diminished precipitation may combine to deplete all the moisture available to plants and the wilting point is reached. The amount of soil moisture available for plant use is essentially the difference between the field capacity and wilting point. 



Soil Properties- Color
The most conspicuous property of a soil is usually its color, but color is by no means the most definitive property. Soil color can provide clues about the nature and capabilities of the soil, but the clues are sometimes misleading. A soil’s color is generally related to its physical and chemical characteristics. Color alone does not answer all the important questions about a soil’s qualities or fertility. Well-known red clay soils of Georgia are not far from Alabama’s belt of black soils. 

Soil scientists recognize 175 gradations of color. The standard colors are generally shades of black, brown, red, yellow, gray, and white. Soil color occasionally reflects the color of unstained mineral grains, but in most cases color is imparted by stains on the surface of the particles. These stains are caused by either metallic oxides or organic matter. 

Black or dark brown usually indicates considerable humus content. The blacker the soil, the more humus it contains. Color gives a strong hint about fertility because humus is an important catalyst in releasing nutrients to plants. Dark color is not invariably a sign of fertility because it may be due to other factors such as poor drainage and high carbonate content. If the humus content of the soil decreases because of either low organic activity or loss of organics through leaching, soil colors typically fade to light gray or brown. 

Reddish and yellowish colors generally indicate iron oxide stains on the outside of soil particles. These colors are most common in tropical and subtropical regions, where many minerals are leached away by water moving under the pull of gravity, leaving insoluble iron compounds behind. In such situations, a red color bespeaks good drainage and a yellowish hue suggests imperfect drainage. Red soils are common in desert and semi desert environments, where the color is carried over intact from the reddish parent materials rather than representing a surface stain. 

Gray and bluish colors typically indicate poor drainage, whereas mottling indicates saturated conditions for part of the year. In humid areas, a light color implies so much leaching that even the iron has been removed, leaving oxides of silicon and aluminum. In dry climates it indicates an accumulation of salts or calcium. It may also indicate simply a lack of organic matter. 

Soil Properties- Texture
All soils are composed of myriad particles of various sizes, although smaller particles usually predominate. Rolling a sample of soil between your fingers can provide a feel for a particular particle size. In this scheme the size groups are called separates. The gravel, sand, and silt separates are fragments of the weathered parent material and are mostly the grains of minerals found in common rock, especially quartz, feldspars, and micas.      

The dominant size is clay particles defined as having diameters of 0.002 millimeters. Silt particles are defined as being between 0.002 and .05 millimeter. Sandy soils have mostly sand sized particles with diameters between 0.05 and 2.0 millimeters. Rocks larger than 2.0 millimeters are regarded as pebbles, gravel, or rock fragments, and technically are not soil particles. 

Soil Profiles
The development of any soil is expressed in two dimensions: depth and time. There is no straight-line relationship between depth and age. Some soils deepen and develop much more rapidly than others. Four processes deepen and age soils: addition (ingredients added to the soil), loss (ingredients lost from the soil), translocation (ingredients moved within the soil), and transformation (ingredients altered within the soil). The five soil-forming factors discussed earlier (geologic, climate, topographic, biological, and time) influence the rate of these four processes. 

The vertical variations of soil properties are not random but rather an ordered layering with depth. Soil tends to have more or less distinctly recognizable layers called horizons, each with different characteristics. The horizons are positioned approximately parallel with the land surface, one above the other. They are separated by a transition zone rather than a sharp line. 

A vertical cross section (as might be seen in a road cut or the side of a trench dug into a field) from the earth’s surface down through the soil layers and into the parent material is referred to as a soil profile. The almost infinite variety of soils in the world are usually grouped and classified on the basis of differences exhibited in their profiles. 

O Horizon (Very Top Soil Layer)
The O Horizon is typically the surface layer, and is organic matter. Both fresh and decaying matter makes up most of the volume. The horizon results essentially from litter derived from dead plants and animals. It is common in forests and generally absent in grasslands. It is actually more typical for soils not to possess an O horizon. The surface horizon for most soils is the A horizon.  

A Horizon (Surface Soil Layer)
The A Horizon is often referred to as topsoil. It is a mineral horizon that contains considerable organic matter. It is formed either at the surface or immediately below an O horizon. A horizons generally contain enough partially decomposed organic matter to give the soil a darker color than underlying horizons. They are normally coarser in texture, having lost some of the finer minerals by erosion and eluviations. Seeds germinate mostly in A horizon.

E Horizon (Second Soil Layer)
The E Horizon is normally lighter in color than either the overlying A or B horizon. It is essentially a layer from which clay, iron, and aluminum have been removed by leaching or eluviation, leaving a concentration of abrasion-resistant sand or silt particles.

B Horizon (Third Soil Layer)
The B Horizon is usually called subsoil. It is a mineral horizon of illuviation where most of the materials removed from above have been deposited. A collecting zone for clay, iron, and aluminum, this horizon is usually of heavier texture, greater density, and relatively greater clay content than the A horizon. 

C Horizon (Fourth Soil Layer)
The C Horizon is unconsolidated parent material (regolith) beyond the reach of the plant’s roots and most soil-forming processes except weathering. It is lacking in organic matter. 

R Horizon (Bottom Layer)
R Horizon is the bedrock with little evidence of weathering.



Time is a critical passive factor in profile development, but the vital active factor is surface water. If there is no surface water from rainfall or snowmelt to infiltrate the soil, there can be no profile development. Descending water carries material from the surface downward (from the top soil to the subsoil) by eluviation and leaching. 

The transported material is mostly deposited a few feet below the surface. In the usual process, topsoil (A) becomes a somewhat depleted horizon through eluviation and leaching. The subsoil (B) develops a layer of accumulation due to illuviation. A profile that contains all horizons is typical of a humid area on well-drained gentle slopes in an environment that has been undisturbed for a long time. In many parts of the world, such idealized conditions do not exist. 

Many soils are too young to have evolved a normal profile. A soil containing only A horizon atop partially altered parent material (C horizon) is said to be immature. The formation of an illuvial B horizon is normally an indication of mature soils.  

Pedogenic Regimes
Soil-forming factors and processes interact in almost limitless variations to produce soils of all descriptions. Fundamental to an understanding of soil classification and distribution is the realization that only five major pedogenic regimes (soil-forming regimes) exist.

Laterization
Laterization is named for the brick-red color of the soil it produces (later is Latin for brick). The processes associated with this regime are typical of the warm, moist regions of the world, and a significant annual moisture surplus is a requisite condition. The soil formed by laterization is most prominent in the tropics and subtropics in regions dominated by forest, shrub, and savanna vegetation.

The laterization regime is characterized by rapid weathering of parent material, dissolution of nearly all minerals and a speedy decomposition of organic matter. The most distinctive feature of laterization is the leaching away of silica (the most common constituent of most rock and soil). Most other minerals are also leached out rapidly, leaving behind primarily iron and aluminum oxides and barren grains of quartz sand. This residue normally imparts to the resulting soil the reddish color that gives this regime its name. 

Because plant litter is rapidly decomposing in places where laterization is the predominate regime, little humus is incorporated into the soil. Even so, plant nutrients are not totally removed by leaching because the natural vegetation (particularly in forests) quickly absorbs many of the nutrients in solution. If the vegetation is relatively undisturbed by human activities, this regime has the most rapid of nutrient cycles and the soil is not totally impoverished by the speed of mineral decomposition and leaching. Where the forest is cleared for agriculture or some other human purpose, most base nutrients are likely to be lost from the cycle because the tree roots that would bring them up are gone. The soil then rapidly becomes impoverished and hard crusts of iron and aluminum compounds are likely to form.  

The general term applied to soils produced by laterization is latosols. These soils sometimes develop to depths of several meters because of strong weathering activities and the fact that laterization continues year-round in these benign climates. Most latosols have little to offer as agricultural soils, but laterization often produces such concentrations of iron and aluminum oxides that mining them can be profitable. 

Podzolization
Podzolization is another regime named after the color of the soil it produces. In this case the soil is gray (podzol is Russian for “like ashes”). It also occurs in regions having a positive moisture balance and involves considerable leaching. Beyond those two characteristics it bears little similarity to laterization. Podzolization occurs primarily in areas where vegetation has limited nutrient requirements and where the plant litter is acidic. These conditions are most prominent in mid and high latitude locales in the Northern Hemisphere with a coniferous forest cover. 

In these cool regions, chemical weathering is slow, but the abundance of acids (plus adequate precipitation) makes leaching very effective. Mechanical weathering from frost action is relatively rapid during the unfrozen part of the year. Much of the land was bulldozed by glaciers, leaving behind an abundance of broken rock debris at the surface. Bedrock here consists mostly of ancient crystalline rocks rich in quartz and aluminum silicates, and poor in alkaline mineral cations important in plant nutrition. 

The boreal forests (dominated by conifers) require little in the way of soil nutrients and their litter returns few nutrient minerals when it decays. The litter is largely needles and twigs, which accumulate on the surface of the soil and decomposes slowly. Microrganisms do not thrive in this environment and so humus production is retarded. Moisture is relatively abundant in summer, so that leaching of whatever nutrient cations are present in the topsoil is relatively complete.

Soil fertility is generally low, and a crumbly structure makes the soil very susceptible to accelerated erosion if the vegetation cover is disturbed, whether by human activities or by natural agencies as wildlife. Soils produced by podzolization often are collectively referred to as podzols. 

Gleization
Gleization is a regime restricted to waterlogged areas, normally in a cool climate. The name comes from glej, Polish for “muddy ground”). Although occasionally widespread, it is generally much more limited in occurrence than laterization and podzolization. The poor drainage that produces a waterlogged environment can be associated with flat land, but it can also result from a topographic depression, a high water table, or various other conditions. In North America, gleization is particularly prominent in areas around the Great Lakes, where recent glacial deposition has interrupted preglacial drainage patterns.

The general term for soils produced by gleization is gley soils. They characteristically have a highly organic A horizon, where decomposition proceeds slowly because bacteria are inhibited by the lack of oxygen in a waterlogged situation. In such anaerobic conditions chemical reduction takes place (notably ferric iron compounds are reduced to ferrous iron compounds). Reduced iron is more easily carried away than oxidized iron, and so over time the soil tends to become poor in iron and gray in color.

Gley soils are usually too acidic and oxygen poor to be productive for anything but water-tolerant vegetation. If drained artificially and fertilized with lime to counteract the acid, their fertility can be greatly enhanced. 

Calcification
 In semiarid and arid climates (where precipitation is less than potential evapotranspiration) leaching is either absent or transitory. Natural vegetation in such areas consists of grasses or shrubs. Calcification (so called because many calcium salts are produced in this regime) is the dominant pedogenic process in these regions, as typified by the drier prairies of North America, the steppes of Eurasia, and the savannas and steppes of the subtropics. 

Both eluviation and leaching are restricted by the absence of percolating water, and so materials that would be carried downward in other regimes become concentrated in the soil where calcification is at work. There is considerable upward movement of water by capillary action in dry periods. Calcium carbonate is the most important chemical compound active in the calcification regime. It is carried downward by limited leaching after a rain and is often concentrated in the B horizon to form a dense layer of hardpan, then brought upward by capillary water and grass roots, and finally returned to the soil when the grass dies.  

Where calcification takes place under undisturbed grassland, the resulting soils are likely to have remarkable agricultural productivity. Humus from decaying grass yields abundant organic colloidal material, imparting a dark color to the soil and contributing to a structure that can retain both nutrients and soil moisture. Where shrubs are the dominant vegetation, roots are fewer but deeper, so nutrients are brought up from deeper layers. Little material accumulates at the surface with less humus incorporated into the soil. 

Where a calcification regime is operative in true deserts, the soils tend to shallower and sandier, little organic matter accumulates either on or in the soil, and the soils are not very different from the parent material. 

Salinization 
In arid and semiarid regions, it is fairly common to find areas with inadequate drainage, particularly in enclosed valleys and basins. Moisture is drawn upward and into the atmosphere by intense evaporation. The evaporating water leaves behind various salts in or on the surface of the soil, sometimes in such quantity as to impart a brilliant white surface color to the land, and the pedogenic regime is called salinization. 

These salts are toxic to most plants and soil organisms, and the resulting soil is able to support very little life apart from a few salt-tolerant grasses and shrubs. Soils developed in a salinization regime can be sometimes made productive through careful water management, but artificial drainage is equally necessary or salt accumulations will be intensified. 

Climate and Pedogenic Regimes
The pedogenic regimes are distinguished primarily on the basis of climate as reflected in temperature and moisture availability and secondarily on the basis of vegetation cover. In regions where there is normally a surplus (which means annual precipitation exceeds annual evapotranspiration) water movement in the soil is predominately downward and leaching is a prominent process. In such areas where temperatures are relatively high throughout the year, laterization is the dominant regime. Where winters are long and cold, podzolization predominates. Where the soil is saturated most of the time due to poor drainage, gleization is notable. 

A broadly valid generalization is that gleization can occur in any pedogenic regimes, as it is more dependent on local topography than on a macroclimate. In regions having a moisture deficit (deserts), the principle soil moisture movement is upward (through capillarity) and leaching is limited. Calcification and salinization are the principle pedogenic regimes in dry conditions.

1. The first step in the formation of soil is weathering, which is the physical and chemical disintegration of rock exposed to the atmosphere and the action of water percolating down from the surface.

2. The five factors of greatest importance in soil formation are geology (parent material), climate (temperature and moisture), topography (slope and drainage), biology (living organisms and decaying organic matter), and time.

3. Earthworms are generally considered beneficial to soils because their activities improve soil structure, increase fertility, lessen the danger of erosion, and deepen the soil profile by mixing and aerating the soil as they tunnel through it.

4. Capillary water is the most important form of soil moisture to plants because it consists of moisture held at the surface of soil particles, readily available for plant roots to absorb along with dissolved nutrients.

5. Soil color can provide clues about its physical and chemical characteristics, such as organic matter content indicated by darker colors, or iron oxide presence indicated by reddish and yellowish hues.

6. Litter is composed of leaves, twigs, stalks, and other dead plant parts accumulating on the soil surface, largely undecomposed. Humus is the decayed remains of plant and animal materials in the soil, partially transformed by microbial action, and plays a fundamental role in supporting plant growth.

7. Temperature is important in the soil-water budget because it influences evaporation and transpiration rates, thereby affecting the amount of water available to plants and the overall moisture balance within the soil.

8. Leaching is the process by which soluble substances and fine particles are dissolved and carried away by water percolating through the soil, potentially depleting the soil of nutrients.

9. Flat areas typically have thicker soil than mountainous areas due to slower erosion rates, allowing more time for soil development processes to occur and for the accumulation of organic matter and weathered material.

10. Soils produced by Calcification typically occur in semiarid and arid climates and are characterized by accumulation of calcium carbonate, dark rich humus from decaying grass yielding organic colloidal material, and a structure capable of retaining both nutrients and soil moisture, making them highly productive agriculturally under undisturbed conditions.
